topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The reception area smelled of sandalwood and expensive paper. I clutched the strap of my worn backpack so tightly my knuckles were white. When the door swung open, I froze mid-stride. Standing there, impeccably dressed and radiating an unnerving calm, was Mark. My Mark. The boy who used to dismantle my lunch money with a casual flick of his wrist. Now, he wore a tailored suit and possessed an air of profound, almost clinical serenity. His eyes, the same sharp grey I remembered, swept over me, and for a split second, I felt every insecurity I’d ever harbored laid bare under a harsh spotlight. My breath hitched, a shallow, useless thing in my chest. I managed a small, strangled sound, a noise somewhere between a gasp and a sob, and immediately tried to pivot away, my feet stumbling over the plush carpet.

[story 2]

It was the waiting room that did it. The silence in the room was heavy, punctuated only by the soft rustle of a magazine. Then, he walked in. He looked… different. The familiar sneer was absent, replaced by an expression of practiced empathy that felt like a physical assault. My stomach clenched into a tight knot, a cold, hard pressure spreading through my abdomen. I watched him approach the intake desk, and every instinct screamed at me to run. My hands began to tremble uncontrollably, the movement so violent I had to press them against my thighs to steady them. When he finally turned, his gaze locked onto mine, and I could feel the blood draining from my face. I opened my mouth to speak, but no sound emerged, just a dry, rasping sensation in my throat.

[story 3]

He sat across from me, leaning forward slightly, his posture open yet somehow impenetrable. I kept my gaze fixed on the pattern of the rug beneath my shoes, focusing intently on the fibers as if they held the secret to survival. Every small movement he made—the way he adjusted his cuff, the slight tilt of his head—sent jolts of alarm through me. My skin felt prickled, as if every nerve ending was suddenly hypersensitive. I swallowed hard, the action difficult and labored, and when I finally forced my eyes up to meet his, I saw not the tormentor, but a master architect of vulnerability. A wave of sheer panic washed over me, leaving me feeling hollowed out, utterly exposed under his scrutiny.
